questions.question: Define a proximity measure. Why is it a fundamental concept in machine learning, and name two common types.
keyPoints: A proximity measure quantifies how similar or dissimilar two data points are.
It is fundamental for tasks like clustering, classification (e.g., k-NN), and anomaly detection, which rely on comparing data instances.
Common types include similarity measures (e.g., Cosine Similarity) and dissimilarity measures (e.g., Euclidean Distance).

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Euclidean distance and Manhattan distance measures. Discuss a scenario where one might be preferred over the other.
keyPoints: Euclidean distance is the straight-line distance between points ('as the crow flies'), calculated as the square root of the sum of squared differences.
Manhattan distance is the sum of the absolute differences along each axis ('city block' distance), representing the path along a grid.

Euclidean is often preferred for physical measurements where a direct path is meaningful. Manhattan can be more robust to outliers and is preferred for high-dimensional sparse data or grid-like paths (e.g., urban navigation).

questions.question: Explain the concept of the 'curse of dimensionality' in the context of proximity measures. Discuss its implications for distance-based algorithms and one strategy to mitigate it.
keyPoints: The curse of dimensionality describes how in high-dimensional spaces, the concept of proximity becomes meaningless because the distance between points becomes increasingly uniform and the volume of the space grows exponentially.
This severely impacts distance-based algorithms like k-NN and k-means clustering, as they rely on meaningful distances to find nearest neighbors or cluster centroids, which fails in high dimensions.
A mitigation strategy is dimensionality reduction (e.g., PCA, Feature Selection) to project data into a lower-dimensional subspace where distances are more meaningful and discriminative.
